exercises.name: The Claim and Proof
exercises.rule: Every claim proven in the same breath.
exercises.why: Builds the habit of carrying evidence with assertion.
exercises.objective: Train the habit of pairing every assertion with immediate evidence, a reason, or a specific example.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: every claim must be paired with evidence in the same breath. Unsupported claims drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Great answers sound like 'X — and here's how I know.' The trap is stacking three claims and promising proof later — later never comes. Weld each claim to one specific number, example, or moment before starting the next sentence.
common_failure_modes: Stacks several claims up front, then back-fills evidence for only the first one
Leans on generic appeals ('studies show', 'everyone knows') instead of a specific example or number
Restates the claim in different words and treats the restatement as proof
Opens with a well-proven point but drifts into bare opinion by the closing claims
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the specificity and proximity of each proof — a concrete example delivered in the same breath outranks a stronger claim proven late or generically.
exercises.retry_objective: Attach proof in the same breath to the claims that floated unsupported in the first attempt.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate arguable, position-taking prompts with a clear audience and stakes that invite multiple assertions, so unsupported claims become visible. Each prompt must suit a 75-120 second spoken response and train claim support only — no stacked constraints.

prompts.text: Argue why our hiring process is broken.
Make the case that remote work hurts junior employees the most.
Argue why startups should never raise from VCs in their first year.
Convince me the four-day workweek improves output, not just morale.
Argue that index funds beat active managers over twenty years.
Make the case that your team's last shipped feature was actually a failure.
Argue that most code reviews don't catch real bugs.
Convince me strength training matters more than cardio for longevity.
Argue why standardized testing predicts college success better than GPA.
Make the case that your company's last reorg destroyed more value than it created.
Argue that microservices were the wrong choice for most teams that adopted them.
Convince me that ozempic is reshaping the food industry, not just healthcare.
Argue that your favorite novel is overrated.
Make the case that nuclear power is the only realistic path to decarbonization.
Argue that the founder should be replaced once a company hits 200 employees.
Convince me that a recession will arrive in the next 18 months.

exercises.name: The Steel Man
exercises.rule: Argue the strongest opposing view before your own.
exercises.why: Forces honest engagement with the other side, not strawmen.
exercises.objective: Train honest engagement with opposing views by articulating the strongest version of the other side before your own.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the response must seriously engage the strongest opposing view before its own. Strawman framing drags thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Argue the other side so well a true believer would say 'exactly.' The trap is a polite one-line summary followed by 'but obviously...' — that's a strawman in a suit. Give their best point real airtime and let it land before you answer it.
common_failure_modes: Compresses the opposing view into one dismissive sentence before pivoting to their own side
Presents the opposition's weakest or most extreme version instead of its best argument
Signals contempt while steel-manning ('some people apparently think...'), undercutting the case being built
Delivers only the opposing case and never returns to their own position
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the opposing case would satisfy an actual proponent — judge its strength and airtime before assessing the speaker's own rebuttal.
exercises.retry_objective: Rebuild the opposing case at full strength — no strawman — before restating your own position.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate genuinely two-sided debate prompts where a strong opposing case exists and the user likely holds a position. Frame the ask as arguing the side the user probably opposes first; each prompt must suit a 75-120 second spoken response.

prompts.text: Make the strongest case AGAINST remote work — even if you support it.
Steel man the argument that AI development should be paused for six months.
Build the strongest case for raising the retirement age to 70.
Make the case for banning short-form video for anyone under 16.
Steel man someone who thinks crypto is a net negative for society.
Build the best argument against the company strategy you currently advocate for.
Steel man the position that college isn't worth it for most students today.
Argue convincingly that your favorite product should not exist.
Make the strongest case for keeping a poor performer who's a culture pillar.
Steel man the view that index investing has gone too far.
Build the strongest case against using LLMs at work.
Argue that single-payer healthcare is a mistake — from the patient's perspective.
Steel man the case for keeping the legacy system instead of rewriting.
Make the strongest argument for arranged marriage.
Steel man the argument that startups should never have an HR department.
Argue that fasting is mostly placebo — even if you fast yourself.

exercises.name: First Principles
exercises.rule: Strip the topic to fundamentals. Build from there.
exercises.why: Bypasses inherited assumptions; reveals what you actually believe.
exercises.objective: Train rebuilding an answer from fundamentals instead of repeating inherited assumptions.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the answer must be rebuilt from fundamentals, not recited convention. Textbook answers and inherited assumptions presented as reasoning drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Real thinking shows when you question the obvious answer out loud — 'we say schools teach, but look at what they actually reward.' The trap is a polished textbook definition; smoothness here usually means the answer is borrowed.
common_failure_modes: Recites the standard textbook answer with fluent confidence and never examines it
Lists surface functions of the thing without asking which one is fundamental
Substitutes jargon or received phrases for reasoning, letting buzzwords stand in for thought
Starts to question an assumption, then retreats to the conventional answer without following the thread
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Reward visible reasoning-in-motion — moments where the speaker tests or discards a conventional answer — over how polished the final definition sounds.
exercises.retry_objective: Strip out the borrowed or conventional reasoning flagged in feedback and rebuild the answer from what is fundamentally true.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate deceptively simple 'why does X exist / what is X for' questions about familiar institutions and practices, where the textbook answer is easy and the first-principles answer requires real thought. Keep prompts short and open; each must suit a 75-120 second spoken response.

prompts.text: Why do companies have org charts?
What is a school for, actually?
Why do we pay engineers more than teachers?
What is money? Answer without using the word currency.
Why does your company exist? Not the mission statement — the actual reason.
What's the point of a performance review? Build the answer from scratch.
Why do hospitals look the way they do?
What is code review really for? Not the textbook answer.
Why do we use email at work?
What is a board of directors fundamentally supposed to do?
Why do humans form romantic pairs at all?
What is a brand, stripped of marketing jargon?
Why do countries have borders? Build the answer from zero.
What is a meeting for? Define it from first principles.
Why do we have intellectual property law?
What is a unit test, fundamentally? Forget the framework.

exercises.name: The So What Test
exercises.rule: Every point answers 'so what' before moving on.
exercises.why: Surfaces stakes — every claim earns its place in the answer.
exercises.objective: Train developing every point to its implication so no statement is left without stakes.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: every point must answer 'so what' before moving on. Listings without stakes drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: After each point, answer the question your listener is silently asking: 'and that matters because...?' The trap is a tidy list that ends where the thinking should start — if a point changes nothing and demands nothing, push it or cut it.
common_failure_modes: Delivers a flat inventory of facts or events with no stakes attached to any item
Attaches an implication to the first point only, then reverts to bare listing
Uses hollow significance phrases ('which is really important') without saying what changes or who should act
Buries the one genuine implication under several stake-free points instead of leading with it
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the depth of implications over the completeness of the recap — three points pushed to consequences beat six points merely reported.
exercises.retry_objective: Take the points that stopped at 'what' and push each one to 'so what' — the implication or action it demands.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate recap, list, and summary-style prompts that tempt the user into flat listing without stakes — the trap is the prompt itself. Everyday professional reporting contexts work best; each prompt must suit a 75-120 second spoken response.

prompts.text: List three things that went well this quarter.
Walk me through your team's latest product launch.
Describe the features in your most recent release.
Recap the key takeaways from the last industry conference you attended.
Summarize your company's Q3 financial results.
Tell me about the new hires you made this year.
Walk through the patient outcomes from your hospital's last initiative.
List the customer feedback themes from this month.
Describe what your team accomplished in the last sprint.
Summarize the curriculum changes at your school this year.
Walk me through the changes in your marketing strategy.
List the AI tools your company has adopted in the last 12 months.
Describe how you spent your weekend working on your side project.
Recap the regulatory changes affecting your industry this year.
Tell me three things you read this week.
Summarize the user research interviews you ran last month.

exercises.name: The Perspective Shift
exercises.rule: Argue from a perspective that is not your own.
exercises.why: Builds the muscle to inhabit another viewpoint, not just attack it.
exercises.objective: Train genuinely inhabiting a viewpoint that is not your own and extracting the insight it reveals.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the response must argue from inside the assigned perspective, not comment on it from the speaker's own seat. Breaking character or caricaturing the lens drags thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Speak as 'I', not 'they' — the CFO says 'I froze hiring because I watch our cash every day,' not 'the CFO probably worries about cash.' The trap is narrating the role from your own seat; commit to the chair and let its real pressures talk.
common_failure_modes: Describes the perspective in third person ('they would argue...') instead of speaking from inside it
Breaks character mid-response to editorialize or signal personal disagreement
Plays the role as caricature, giving it shallow or cartoonish motives
Argues generic points anyone could make rather than the concerns specific to that seat
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight sustained immersion — whether the assigned seat's specific pressures and language hold from the first sentence to the last, not just in the opening.
exercises.retry_objective: Re-enter the assigned perspective fully — argue from inside it instead of commenting on it from your own seat.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate prompts that name an explicit perspective the user must argue from — a role, seat, or worldview plausibly opposed to their own. The assigned lens should create real friction with the user's likely view; each prompt must suit a 75-120 second spoken response.

prompts.text: Defend the CFO's decision to freeze hiring — from the CFO's chair.
Argue as the engineer being asked to ship before it's ready.
Defend the customer who just churned — from their perspective.
Argue as the teacher whose classroom you'd never want to teach in.
Defend a return-to-office mandate — from the CEO's seat.
Argue as the nurse on a 12-hour shift, defending the slow process you'd reform.
Defend the journalist who wrote the story you hated.
Argue as a regulator pushing the rule your industry is fighting.
Defend the founder who took the acquisition you'd have refused.
Argue as the parent of the student whose teacher you'd fire.
Defend the doctor who recommended the procedure you wouldn't have chosen.
Argue as the politician you most disagree with — make their best speech.
Defend the artist whose work you can't stand.
Argue as the PM who killed the feature your team built.
Defend your partner's side of the last fight you had.
Argue as the investor who passed on your company.

exercises.name: Draw the Line
exercises.rule: Name the limit of your argument explicitly.
exercises.why: Calibrated confidence — saying where you stop knowing is a power move.
exercises.objective: Train calibrated confidence by stating explicitly where your argument or prediction stops being reliable.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the response must state explicitly where its confidence ends. Predictions delivered without a named limit or uncertainty drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Strong forecasters split the answer: 'here's what I'm confident about, here's where I'm guessing.' The trap is hedging everything or hedging nothing — one named boundary ('past two years, I'm speculating') beats ten scattered maybes.
common_failure_modes: Delivers the whole prediction with uniform confidence and never marks where knowledge ends
Sprinkles reflexive hedges ('maybe', 'I guess') throughout instead of drawing one clear boundary
Declares blanket uncertainty ('nobody can know') without separating what they do know from what they don't
Tacks a limit on at the very end as a throwaway disclaimer rather than building the answer around it
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the precision and placement of the confidence boundary — a specific, located limit counts more than generic disclaimers or the boldness of the forecast itself.
exercises.retry_objective: Restate the prediction with an explicit confidence boundary — what you know, what you are guessing, and where the line sits.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate forecast and prediction prompts with genuine uncertainty — future-dated, partly unknowable questions where overconfidence is the natural failure. Each prompt must suit a 75-120 second spoken response.

prompts.text: Will AI replace software engineers in the next decade?
Where will interest rates be in 18 months?
Will your company exist in five years? Where does your confidence end?
Predict whether remote work share rises or falls by 2030.
Will electric vehicles cross 50% of new car sales in the US by 2032?
Forecast the next major shift in social media platforms.
Will quantum computing meaningfully impact business by 2035?
Predict the next big healthcare breakthrough.
Will the four-day workweek be standard by 2035?
Forecast which industry will be most disrupted by AI agents in three years.
Will universal basic income exist in any G7 country within 15 years?
Predict your own career trajectory five years out.
Will the next iPhone be a meaningful upgrade?
Forecast whether your industry consolidates or fragments over the next decade.
Will K-12 schools still grade students by 2040?
Predict the next recession's trigger.

exercises.name: Name the Assumption
exercises.rule: Surface the load-bearing assumption before the conclusion.
exercises.why: Most disagreements are about assumptions, not conclusions. Surface them.
exercises.objective: Train surfacing the load-bearing assumption underneath a recommendation before delivering the conclusion.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the load-bearing assumption must be surfaced before the conclusion. Recommendations whose assumptions stay hidden drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Say the bet out loud before the pitch: 'this only works if enterprise buyers behave like our SMB customers do.' The trap is selling benefits — the pitch is the easy part; naming the one assumption that could sink it is the exercise.
common_failure_modes: Argues the recommendation's benefits and never surfaces any assumption at all
Names a trivial or safe assumption while the real load-bearing bet stays hidden
Mentions the assumption only after the conclusion, as an afterthought instead of a foundation
Lists several assumptions without identifying which one the case actually stands or falls on
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the surfaced assumption is genuinely load-bearing — the one the conclusion collapses without — and whether it arrives before the pitch, not after.
exercises.retry_objective: Name the hidden assumption the first attempt built on, then show how the conclusion depends on it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate recommendation and advocacy prompts ('argue we should X') whose case silently rests on one or two big assumptions — market, people, or timing bets the user must surface. Each prompt must suit a 75-120 second spoken response.

prompts.text: Argue we should expand to Europe next year.
Make the case for rewriting the codebase in a new language.
Argue that your team needs to double headcount.
Make the case to acquire your top competitor.
Argue that the company should go public next year.
Make the case for switching the company's pricing model to usage-based.
Argue we should sunset our most-used legacy feature.
Make the case for moving your headquarters out of the city.
Argue that your school should drop letter grades entirely.
Make the case for a hospital to adopt AI-led triage.
Argue we should kill the marketing team and replace it with content creators.
Make the case to invest your company's cash reserves in equities.
Argue that your product needs to be free for the first year.
Make the case to merge two teams that have always operated separately.
Argue we should outsource customer support to AI agents this year.
Make the case for moving in with your partner after six months.

exercises.name: Disconfirm Yourself
exercises.rule: State what would change your mind.
exercises.why: Proof you've actually thought about the other side, not just dismissed it.
exercises.objective: Train falsifiable thinking by stating concretely what evidence would change your mind.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the response must name concrete, observable evidence that would change the speaker's mind. Vague or unfalsifiable disconfirmers drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Name evidence you could actually see: 'if churn rises two quarters straight after we go remote, I'm wrong.' The trap is defending the belief or vague outs like 'strong evidence would sway me' — that's a test nothing can fail.
common_failure_modes: Spends the response defending the belief instead of naming what would disprove it
Offers unfalsifiable disconfirmers ('if it stopped working', 'overwhelming evidence') no observation could trigger
Names evidence so extreme or unlikely that the belief is effectively untouchable
Gives a disconfirmer for a weaker nearby claim while leaving the actual belief untested
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight how observable and realistic the disconfirming evidence is — could a third party check it and call the flip — rather than how well the belief itself is argued.
exercises.retry_objective: Replace vague disconfirmers with specific, observable evidence that would actually flip the belief.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate 'you believe X — what would prove you wrong?' prompts anchored in beliefs the user plausibly holds about work, money, or life. The prompt must demand disconfirming evidence, not a defense of the belief; each prompt must suit a 75-120 second spoken response.

prompts.text: You believe remote work makes teams more productive — what would prove you wrong?
You think your company's strategy is the right one. What evidence would flip you?
You believe LLMs will keep getting smarter. What would make you stop believing that?
You think your best engineer is irreplaceable. What would change your mind?
You believe index funds beat active managers. What data would shake that?
You think a college degree is worth the cost. What would convince you it isn't?
You believe your hiring bar is high enough. What would prove it isn't?
You think your relationship is healthy. What would tell you it isn't?
You believe nuclear power is safe. What event or finding would change that?
You think your product is loved by users. What would tell you it isn't?
You believe the housing market in your city is overvalued. What would prove you wrong?
You think you'd be a good founder. What evidence would tell you you wouldn't?
You believe your parenting approach is working. What would tell you it's not?
You think AI is a bubble. What would tell you it's not?
You believe your favorite leader is the right one for the country. What would change that?
You think your diet is healthy. What evidence would make you change it?

exercises.name: Order of Magnitude
exercises.rule: Commit to a rough number. No 'many' or 'some'.
exercises.why: Forces real estimation. Vague quantifiers are a thinking-quality tell.
exercises.objective: Train committing to a concrete numerical estimate and showing the reasoning path behind it.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the response must commit to a specific number and show the estimation path. Vague quantifiers (many, some, a lot) drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Commit to a number early, then show your math out loud: 'call it 40 engineers — roughly one per million of ARR, plus a platform team.' The trap is talking around it; 'it depends' and 'quite a few' are exactly what this rep exists to kill.
common_failure_modes: Never commits to a number, hiding behind 'it depends' or vague quantifiers like 'a lot'
States a number with no visible estimation path — a guess dressed up as an answer
Gives an unusably wide range ('somewhere between 10 and 500') to avoid real commitment
Spends the response caveating the question instead of building toward an estimate
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the reasoning chain from anchor to answer — a defensible path to an imperfect number scores above a lucky number with no visible path.
exercises.retry_objective: Replace the vague quantifiers with a committed number and walk the estimation steps that produce it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate 'how many / how much' estimation questions with no easily lookup-able answer, where a defensible order-of-magnitude number must be reasoned out loud. Prompts should be answerable only by committing to a number, never by 'it depends' alone; each must suit a 75-120 second spoken response.

prompts.text: How many engineers does a company need to ship and maintain a SaaS product at $50M ARR?
How much should a Series A startup spend on marketing in year one?
How many hours per week does your average knowledge worker actually focus?
How big a market does an early-stage VC need to see to invest?
How many users does a consumer app need before it's worth monetizing?
How much should a hospital spend on cybersecurity per bed per year?
How many students per teacher is the right ratio in a public high school?
How many parameters does an LLM need to be useful for a business?
How much runway should a startup keep before raising again?
How long does it take a new exec to become net positive?
How many one-on-ones should a manager run per week before burning out?
How much electricity does a single ChatGPT query use compared to a Google search?
How many doctors does a city of one million people actually need?
How much should you save by age 40 to retire at 65?
How many product features should ship in a year for a 50-person startup?
How many dates before you know if it's a real relationship?
